sources.employment: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook (OOH), 2024 data — includes self-employed. Where BLS combines occupations on OOH pages, granular figures from BLS Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics (OES), May 2023 are used instead.
sources.wages: BLS OOH 2024 median annual wages where available. For occupations where BLS reports hourly only (Actors, Announcers), annual estimated at hourly x 2,080. For OES-sourced occupations, median annual estimated from median hourly x 2,080.
sources.aiScores: Generated by Claude (Anthropic) — see methodology in README
sources.newspaperDecline: Northwestern Medill State of Local News 2024, Pew Research Center

notes.oesSourced: PR Managers, Advertising Managers, Broadcast Technicians, Sound Engineers, Film/Video Editors, Camera Operators, and Proofreaders use OES May 2023 data because their OOH pages combine them with unrelated occupations. OES counts wage-and-salary workers only (excludes self-employed), so these figures undercount total workforce.
notes.combinedOccupations: BLS OOH combines: PR + Fundraising Managers; Advertising + Promotions + Marketing Managers; Broadcast + Sound + Video Technicians; Film/Video Editors + Camera Operators. We report each role separately using granular OES data.

occupations.title: Reporters & Correspondents
occupations.aiRationale: Core output is text generation from information sources — exactly what LLMs do. Wire service copy, breaking news summaries, and routine coverage are already being automated.

occupations.title: Editors
occupations.aiRationale: Structural editing, copy editing, headline writing, and content curation are increasingly handled by AI tools. Human editors shift toward taste-making and judgment roles.

occupations.title: Technical Writers
occupations.aiRationale: Documentation, API references, and instructional content are among the first outputs AI can produce reliably from code and specifications.

occupations.title: Writers & Authors
occupations.aiRationale: Commercial and genre writing face high displacement. Literary and deeply personal writing retains value. The middle — content, ghostwriting — is contested territory.

occupations.title: Proofreaders & Copy Markers
occupations.aiRationale: Grammar, consistency, and style checking are fully automatable. AI already outperforms human proofreaders on speed and catch-rate for mechanical errors. Terminal decline.
occupations.blsNote: No individual OOH page exists. Employment from OES May 2023 (wage-and-salary only).

occupations.title: Interpreters & Translators
occupations.aiRationale: Machine translation has reached near-human quality for major language pairs. Live interpretation and nuanced cultural translation still require humans.

occupations.title: Public Relations Specialists
occupations.aiRationale: Press release drafting, media monitoring, and pitch writing are automatable. Strategic relationship management and crisis communications are harder to replace.

occupations.title: Public Relations Managers
occupations.aiRationale: Strategic direction, stakeholder relationships, and organizational judgment are AI-resistant. The managerial layer itself is not at risk of elimination.
occupations.blsNote: OOH page combines PR + Fundraising Managers (128,900 total). Employment shown is PR Managers only (SOC 11-2032) from OES May 2023.

occupations.title: Advertising & Promotions Managers
occupations.aiRationale: Campaign strategy and brand judgment remain human. AI automates ad copy, A/B testing, audience segmentation, and performance optimization — compressing team headcount.
occupations.blsNote: OOH page combines Advertising + Promotions + Marketing Managers (434,000 total). Employment shown is Advertising & Promotions Managers only (SOC 11-2011) from OES May 2023.

occupations.title: Market Research Analysts
occupations.aiRationale: Data synthesis, survey analysis, and report writing are automatable. Research design and translating ambiguous business questions into studies still require humans.

occupations.title: Announcers
occupations.aiRationale: Voice synthesis has reached broadcast quality. Sports summaries, traffic reports, and routine announcements are being replaced. Live event hosting retains human presence value.

occupations.title: Broadcast Technicians
occupations.aiRationale: Physical equipment operation, live event management, and real-time problem-solving are durable. AI-assisted mastering and automated monitoring reduce headcount for routine operations.
occupations.blsNote: OOH page combines Broadcast + Sound + Video Technicians (146,100 total). Employment shown is Broadcast Technicians only (SOC 27-4012) from OES May 2023.

occupations.title: Sound Engineering Technicians
occupations.aiRationale: Live mixing and studio work require trained ears and collaboration. AI handles mastering and noise reduction well, but creative sonic decisions remain human.
occupations.blsNote: OOH page combines Broadcast + Sound + Video Technicians (146,100 total). Employment shown is Sound Engineering Technicians only (SOC 27-4014) from OES May 2023.

occupations.title: Film & Video Editors
occupations.aiRationale: AI-powered rough-cut assembly is automating the most time-consuming phase. Creative editorial judgment, pacing, and narrative shaping remain human. Juniors most at risk.
occupations.blsNote: OOH page combines Film/Video Editors + Camera Operators (79,900 total). Employment shown is Film and Video Editors only (SOC 27-4032) from OES May 2023.

occupations.title: Producers & Directors
occupations.aiRationale: Creative vision, talent management, and production orchestration require human judgment and relationships. AI augments but doesn't replace the orchestrator role.

occupations.title: Camera Operators
occupations.aiRationale: Requires physical presence, creative framing, and director collaboration. AI-assisted tracking and drones handle specific use cases but haven't displaced professional camera work.
occupations.blsNote: OOH page combines Film/Video Editors + Camera Operators (79,900 total). Employment shown is Camera Operators only (SOC 27-4031) from OES May 2023.

occupations.title: Special Effects Artists & Animators
occupations.aiRationale: Generative AI is compressing production timelines and reducing junior headcount. High-end character animation, rigging, and art direction remain human-led.

occupations.title: Graphic Designers
occupations.aiRationale: Canva AI, Firefly, and Midjourney have automated commodity design. Strategic brand design and complex visual systems remain human. Volume work is under serious pressure.

occupations.title: Art Directors
occupations.aiRationale: Creative direction requires taste, cultural awareness, and stakeholder alignment. AI generates executions; art directors define what should be executed.

occupations.title: Photographers
occupations.aiRationale: Stock photography displaced by generative AI at scale. Editorial and news photography retain value (presence matters). Portrait and event photography is experience-driven.

occupations.title: Actors
occupations.aiRationale: Digital doubles and voice cloning raise consent issues. Live performance and cultural value of human artistry maintain relevance. SAG-AFTRA contracts now address AI protections.
occupations.blsNote: BLS reports hourly wage only ($23.33/hr). Annual estimate assumes 2,080 hours; actual earnings vary widely due to irregular work schedules.

occupations.title: Survey Researchers
occupations.aiRationale: Survey design, data cleaning, and report synthesis are automatable. AI can generate, deploy, and analyze surveys faster than human teams.
